@qwexvf Thank you for the detailed info.
Related code is here and it is only used by pubsub(for now) to register a callback when connection is connected and then re-subscribe to any channels and patterns previously subscribed to.

The reason why pubsub can still run smoothly because it is a warning and only the re-subscription is influenced(which may be not so obvious).

```Python
async def connect(self):
        try:
            await self._connect()
        except Exception as exc:
            raise ConnectionError()
        # run any user callbacks. right now the only internal callback
        # is for pubsub channel/pattern resubscription
        for callback in self._connect_callbacks:
            # this is the coroutine which is not awaited
            task = callback(self)
            if isinstance(task, types.CoroutineType):
                await task
```
It is weird that `Pubsub.on_connect` is not awaited, i will check it now.

I haven't looked at the generated `zdesk_api.py` yet, but your contributions look good. I have two items we should consider.

One, I'm a little wary on this change:

https://github.com/blade2005/zdesk/blob/cb0b9f59e81fa88e17867b6f312a381abf9d0a0f/api_gen/api_gen.py#L213

The original three `del`s work explicitly on the first three items in the list. We know what they contain and that we don't want them. For the sake of avoiding future, hard to debug problems, I'm interested in keeping it that way. The generator expression is elegant and compact, but also will work to remove `'', 'api', 'v2'` from other path components than just the first three, which would be a bug.

The only other thing that I am wondering is if, since you addressed one file write encoding problem, we shouldn't also add such an encoding to the resulting `zdesk_api.py` write here:

https://github.com/blade2005/zdesk/blob/cb0b9f59e81fa88e17867b6f312a381abf9d0a0f/api_gen/api_gen.py#L586

Also, I generally make changes to `api_gen.py` and commit those, then do the regeneration of `zdesk_api.py` as a separate commit. This allows the users to reference the commit on that file to see what changed just in the API usage, and also I can make the commit message a summary of the changes. See this for an example:

https://github.com/fprimex/zdesk/commit/bb455aeac4ffb9c7a6f5cabb9653cf46cdcb8531

These commits are provided in the [CHANGELOG.md](https://github.com/fprimex/zdesk/blob/master/CHANGELOG.md):

> Regenerate API from updated mirror. See [full commit](https://github.com/fprimex/zdesk/commit/bb455aeac4ffb9c7a6f5cabb9653cf46cdcb8531)

I don't know if you're interested in splitting your commits up, or putting in the work to diff and scrutinize the results of the `zdesk_api.py` commit, but I'm willing to handle the `zdesk_api.py` commit with the extensive commentary if you can remove that file change from your PR.

Hi, I started to work on a asyncio ldap3 server project (sldap3 on github) using ldap3 but I stopped when I found that it was not possible to wrap a plain socket in a ssl socket in the main loop. I created an issue on the python bug tracking  site (https://bugs.python.org/issue23749). Wrapping a socket with sleep is needed for the start_tls operation. 

Anyway I think you should subclass the SyncStrategy connection and yield from the send and receive methods, but I'm quite new to async programming, so I'm not sure if this is the correct approach.

Anyway I would be happy to help and include a new strategy in the ldap3 library.

https://github.com/davisking/dlib/search?q=cudaMalloc&unscoped_q=cudaMalloc returns these two `cudaMalloc`:
https://github.com/davisking/dlib/blob/24ac9bc43f62e412bfc132ba2966b7c7f99e5349/dlib/cuda/cuda_data_ptr.cpp#L28
https://github.com/davisking/dlib/blob/24ac9bc43f62e412bfc132ba2966b7c7f99e5349/dlib/cuda/gpu_data.cpp#L195

Even if you discount the logging after the first cudaMalloc error, there are more `cudaMalloc` calls than `cudaFree` calls.

I'm not sure what to do about this one. I can think of lots of different ways to handle it:

1. A separate keyring backend for Mac OS X internet keys.
2. A attribute in the keyring that can be mutated to indicate to use internet keyring.
3. A config parameter that indicates which keyring to use.
4. A separate parameter in the get/set/delete password calls to indicate which variant of the keyring to use.
5. A special indicator parsed from the 'system' that indicates which variant to use.

I don't like (1) at all, and listed it here mostly for completeness. I'm leaning a little bit toward (4), but the main thing that holds me back is that a client that's not aware of the variant wouldn't be able to select it readily.

I believe (5) is akin to the suggestion to use a URL. I'm open to suggestions on this one.

1. Ok, @itsucks your problem:

- receivefile.py is not always acknowledging EOT (End of Transmission)?

I don't believe to be a problem on our side -- I'm very certain you are not "flushing" data -- that is, waiting for the remote end to receive all bytes that have been sent. a sleep for 1 second isn't perfect, but it serves the same ends.

Hey, by the way, do you know that XMODEM performs "padding" on files to 128-byte blocks?

So if you had a 96-byte .txt file, it would become 128 bytes -- the remaining 32 bytes are "padded" as 32 bytes of b'\x1a' (Ctrl + Z). This is the argument `pad` to XMODEM initializer.

You might not notice this in your text editor, but it happens. In the old days (CP/M and MS-DOS) ^Z has significance for text files to mean "End of file", and the XMODEM protocol is very old in this regard ...

So... I would recommend you do something out of band to indicate the final size of the file and "trim" away those paddings -- if you wanted to transfer any kind of binary file, it would likely become corrupt, otherwise.

> Won't setting the data-dir parameter override that default dir you are already using?

There is the [default](https://github.com/etingof/snmpsim/blob/master/snmpsim/confdir.py#L47) data files search list in which `--data-dir` would be  added in front of the default ones ([unless](https://github.com/etingof/snmpsim/blob/master/scripts/snmpsimd.py#L628) you configure many SNMP engines).

> I also see that community has to be specifically referenced. 

Well, there is usually some community name when in comes to SNMP v1/v2c. The universally used one is 'public' so you may consider [symlinking](http://snmpsim.sourceforge.net/sharing-snapshots.html)  *public.snmprec -> variation/notification.snmprec* and/or *1.3.6.1.6.1.1.0.snmprec -> public.snmprec* to reuse them across different simulated agents. In the latter case you could probably use empty community name.

With SNMPv3 there is `context-name` to identify simulated agent so it also maps to .snmprec files.. The `context-name` could be empty.

> How should I signal them for a specific engine/user

SNMPv3 users do not take part in .snmprec file selection, just SNMPv3 `context-name`.

Hey, thanks for reporting this! The issue is the query cache. We cache the results of queries as long as the data doesn't change. Now, we store the query cache _per table instance_. You have two table instances and if you delete an element in one, the other query cache wont't get updated.

I'm really not sure how we could solve this (besides making the documentation say that such behavior is unsafe). We would need to have a global query cache instead of a per table one. We'd have to separate the queries we cache by table name. But the user might have multiple database instances with different files which still share table names, so we would have to account for the storage source as well which isn't supported in the current design. I think, I'll have to think about this for some time…

Okay, so as I started looking into how to actually implement this, I started to discover a few things that would make it strange to work with.

1. You'd have no control over how many futures would complete before control is returned to your `select` code block, which is weird compared to how you'd normally use `select` in other languages (or even python's own `select()`. So a low-level function that is supposed to return when _the first_ futures completes seems impossible without touching internals.
2. As you are not asking for what is _ready_ (as with the normal select) but what _completed_, you wouldn't be able to just not care what happened to the remaining tasks. It would be a mess of weird corner cases.
3. I then tried to work out if it would make sense to do it like a normal select (to tell you when something is _ready_ for a write or a read) but that would require hooking in to the existing waiters (the pending putters and getters), which could mess up the queue if the external handler does something bad.

So, it doesn't seem like using the existing async interface is a good way to provide this functionality.

I think the best way to get something select-like would be to hook into the existing waiters, I'll just have to think of a way to do this without making it super easy to break a queue by binding a bad handler.


Oh, and the previous scenario we were toying with can easily solved like this instead:

(full working example)

```python
import asyncio
from aiochannel import Channel


async def work(numbers, control, out):
    # work's shared context
    class Context(object):
        multiplier = 0

    # how to handle control data
    async def handle_control(ctx):
        async for n in control:
            print("control: {}".format(n))
            ctx.multiplier = n
        numbers.close()

    # how to handle number data
    async def handle_number(ctx):
        async for n in numbers:
            print("number:  {} * {}".format(n, ctx.multiplier))
            value = ctx.multiplier * n
            await out.put(value)
        control.close()

    # create shared context
    context = Context()
    # wait for jobs to complete
    await asyncio.wait([handle_control(context), handle_number(context)])
    # close out channel
    out.close()


async def main(loop):
    numbers = Channel()
    control = Channel()
    out = Channel()

    # demo data
    async def number_adder():
        for i in range(20):
            await asyncio.sleep(0.05)
            await numbers.put(i)
        numbers.close()

    async def control_adder():
        for i in range(1, 4):
            await asyncio.sleep(0.2)
            await control.put(i)

    asyncio.ensure_future(number_adder())
    asyncio.ensure_future(control_adder())


    # wait for the work to be done
    await work(numbers, control, out)

    # and print the channel's internal queue 
    print(list(out._queue))


loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
loop.run_until_complete(main(loop))
loop.close()
```

which is actually about the same size as the other examples, and I think it reads better.

> Quite frequently, ground-truth annotations contain leading and/or trailing silence, typically (e.g. in SALAMI) annotated using special segment labels like 'Silence'. When such a special segment label appears at the front, the start time of this segment does not contain a real boundary which boundary detection algorithms would find, conversely for ending times of silent end segments.
> 
> Do you mean the start or the end of the first segment?
> 
> 
The start of the first segment, or conversely, the end of the last segment.

> In a well-formed annotation, the start time should be 0, which I've argued should never be counted as positive hits. Setting trim=True will discount the first and last segment boundaries: the only reason it's set to False is backwards compatibility against mirex by default.
> 
> 

Exactly, but this is difficult terrain. In SALAMI, the two following variations appear equally often, here shown with (start,end,label) triplets:

(0, 0.3, 'Silence'), (0.3, 10, 'A'), .... , (120, 130, 'C'), (130, 131, 'Silence')
or
(0, 10, 'A'), .... , (120, 131, 'C')

In the first case, the annotator was very careful checking leading and trailing silence (the latter probably being a fade-out), in the second case the annotator was less pedantic.

Trimming both versions in segment.detection yields completely different sets of reference boundaries.
 
Let's assume that a typical good boundary detector would detect boundary estimates at approximately 0 seconds and somewhere between 130 and 131 seconds, comparing two the two trimmed reference sets would result in very different evaluations.
Right now, segment.detection is not aware of the fact that a leading or trailing part labeled 'Silence' must be handled differently than a non-silent part.
> Currently, it seems that the segment.detection function must be tricked by introducing very short silent segments, if those are not already present in the ground-truth, to consistently trim them, or not.
> 
> I'm not sure what you mean here: are you talking about modifying the reference annotations? Or the estimated annotations? I don't see why either should be necessary.
> 
> 

If you look at the two examples, the second one missing leading and trailing silences could be converted to something like
(0,0.001, 'Silence'), (0.001, 10, 'A'), .... , (120, 130.199, 'C'),(130.999,131, 'Silence')
so that both variations would be the same after trimming.

It looks like the underlying issue here has to do with how curses optimizes screen refreshes. Since tmux is a virtual termial, it can only support a subset of the commands that a full terminal can. That's why it uses the same [terminfo](https://linux.die.net/man/5/terminfo) entry as screen (an even older virtual terminal) to define which commands are valid. When tmux's $TERM set to xterm, curses must incorrectly assume that it has access to some command (like bce, background color erase) so it sends an invalid escape code that screws up the rendering.

I was able to fix by swapping out curses.touchwin() with curses.clearok(). The former tries to optimize drawing to the screen by only sending the characters that have changed since the previous refresh. The latter clears the memory and sends the whole screen on each refresh. I don't know exactly what this changes under the curses hood but it seems to do the trick for me.

Let the debate begin! 

It is an issue I think with a practical application here, but a much bigger design pattern discussion. I'm not sure I agree that try...except... is for rarity only.

In fact, the debate between asking permission/forgiveness is baked into python itself and has been for many years.

> Easier to ask for forgiveness than permission. This common Python coding style assumes the existence of valid keys or attributes and catches exceptions if the assumption proves false. This clean and fast style is characterized by the presence of many try and except statements. The technique contrasts with the LBYL style common to many other languages such as C.

https://docs.python.org/3.6/glossary.html#term-eafp

So I understand is_authenticated may be a misleading term. But I'm not sure I agree that the application should not throw exceptions. The exception is a great way to pull the escape lever and let it trickle down and be known that authentication failed as opposed to carrying that thru is some sort of state.

def OnKeyEvent(self, browser, event, eventHandle):
...
...
        if get_platform()==PLATFORM_MAC:
            #the command button is 128

                if event["native_key_code"]==8:
                    #c char
                    #copy the data
                    mainBrowser.GetFocusedFrame().copy()
                elif event["native_key_code"]==9:
                    #v char
                   #paste the data
                    mainBrowser.GetFocusedFrame().paste()

...
...

Basically angular will replace ``:param_name`` with passed kwargs or object attribute if there's one with such name.

So if we build a url like ``/angular/reverse/?djng_url_name=orders%3Aorder_buyer_detail&djng_url_kwarg_pk=:pk`` it will work ok when angular replaces ``:pk`` part with e.g. ``17``.
But if it isn't replace we will get ``{'pk': ':pk'}`` in django middleware. I guess we could ignore params starting with ``:``.

Then you can't use kwargs/args that start with ``:`` which shouldn't be a problem really. Thoughts?

Names starting with underscore by Python's convention is protected/private symbols which those matchers are not. All uppercase is for constants, not functions. That leaves us with names ending with underscore.

You're right, this is sphinx complaining. Presumably breate is doing the equivalent of writing
```
.. cpp:var:: template messageHandlers<Go>::type messageHandlers<Go>::handler
```
for me, which gives the same error.

I don't really want to dig too deep into this, but either:
* Doxygen is outputting the `template` keyword, when it should probably throw it away it stores that data elsewhere (that it is a variable). Solution: Patch doxygen
* Doxygen and sphinx are both working to spec. Solution: Strip this keyword inside breathe
* Sphinx missed a case in its parsing of `:cpp:var:`. Solution: Patch sphinx

So the only real "bug" in breathe here is being a poor middle man between two possibly-broken tools.

        question = request.json['question']
        response = chatbot.get_response(question)

        return jsonify({

@saper The `Content-MD5` header should be set to the md5 checksum of the file being uploaded (if provided). Once all of the data has been handed off to archive.org, archive.org will calculate an md5 checkusm of the data and check to see if it matches the checkusm in the `Content-MD5` header. If it does, archive.org returns a 200 and your item gets created. If they do not match, a 400 status is returned and no data is saved. For example: ``` » curl --location --header 'x-amz-auto-make-bucket:1' \ 2016-01-11 19:33:12 --header 'Content-MD5:bad md5' \ --header 'x-archive-meta-mediatype:texts' \ --header 'x-archive-meta-collection:test_collection' \ --header 'x-archive-meta-language:eng' \ --header "authorization: LOW $access:$secret" \ --upload-file ./1866_Muller_A928.pdf \ http://s3.us.archive.org/jake-s3-test-2016-01-11/1866_Muller_A928.pdf BadDigestThe Content-MD5 you specified did not match what we received.content-md5 submitted with PUT: bad md5 != recieved data md5: 9a021f2e21b24a9641a35758cb6f83f5also not equal to base64 version: mgIfLiGySpZBo1dYy2+D9Q==e1c3950a-752b-4905-9cdf-bd28c1e6702a⏎ ``` See [RFC 1864](https://www.ietf.org/rfc/rfc1864.txt) for more details on the `Content-MD5` header. I would love to get `cat data | ia upload - --remote-name file.txt` working properly (i.e. stream from stdin rather than read it all into memory). I have not worked with or looked at the upload from stdin code in a while, if you'd like to work on this @saper that would be great! I also love the idea of being able to specify an md5 via an arg. I think in the Python side of things we should check for a `Content-MD5` header in `upload()` and if one exists use that for the md5 rather than calculating one. The CLI should have an `--md5` option that will be handed off to `upload()` as a `Content-MD5` header.

quick thoughts on accumulators with multiprocessing: 
* need a list of all instantiated accumulators in `Context._accumulators = []` (or something similar)
* send all accumulators out to workers in `Context.run_job()`
* every worker needs to start its accumulators at "zero" value (could be done in the deserialized version on the worker)
* workers need to return their accumulators (same way `stats` are returned): https://github.com/svenkreiss/pysparkling/blob/master/pysparkling/context.py#L55
* on aggregation, add accumulators from the workers back to original (about here: https://github.com/svenkreiss/pysparkling/blob/master/pysparkling/context.py#L287)
* here https://github.com/svenkreiss/pysparkling/blob/master/pysparkling/context.py#L287, the `s` that gets returned is for "stats" of that worker. That whole "stats" mechanism should be rewritten with accumulators.

The idea would basically be to replace the whole `stats` mechanism with accumulators and implement `stats` with accumulators.

The [server-sent events specification](https://html.spec.whatwg.org/multipage/server-sent-events.html#server-sent-events) includes a way to implement a mechanism like that: the `Last-Event-ID` header. When the client connects to the server's event stream, it should be able to provide this header, which tells the server what was the last event that the client saw. The server can then immediately return all the events that have happened since then, and continue streaming events into the future.

However, Flask-SSE does not support this mechanism. In order to support it, you'll need several pieces of functionality:

- A store that can hold on to events, with some rolling window -- perhaps holding the last 5 minutes of events, for example
- A way of assigning IDs to events (timestamps would probably work)
- A way of fetching all events from the store before a certain ID

Once you have all of that, you should be able to subclass the `ServerSentEventsBlueprint` and override the `stream()` method to fetch events from the store before connecting to the `messages()` generator.

What backend are you using? If s3boto, the problem might be that the default is `AWS_S3_FILE_OVERWRITE = True`. This makes sense for static files but not for media files.

You probably want a separate storage class for media, something like this:

```python
class MediaS3BotoStorage(S3BotoStorage):
     location = 'media'
     file_overwrite = False  # override default AWS_S3_FILE_OVERWRITE=True
```

import PySimpleGUI as sg

It's not normal to implement the login view yourself. Instead, you include
the authentication views in your project like this:

https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.11/topics/auth/default/#module-django.contrib.auth.views

Then any view that requires a login you decorate with the login_required()
decorator:

https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.11/topics/auth/default/#the-login-required-decorator

So you should never be calling login() yourself.

For your search view, you shouldn't be using authenticate(), as that
requires both a username and a password. Instead, you should be using a raw
LDAP connection to look up a user by username. There are some undocumented
helpers in django_python3_ldap that might help you out. Something like this:

https://gist.github.com/etianen/b7535ca5c6f45efa3b540bb92ae8b518

I haven't tested that code, so there may be typos, but it's a start.

OK so you can exclude them which may make things very difficult given the normal Django behavior is to GROUP BY on all fields from the model object being used for the aggregates.

A way around the error is to convert the text field into a varchar.

E.G.

`Convert(Varchar(<max length>),<column name>) AS <column name>`

The drawback is truncation of the field which may cause odd side effects if the field in question has multiple values that truncate to the same value when limited to the max varchar length. That would cause the aggregates to break anyways.

Hi and thanks for the pull request! I just merged this and published the result as rotate-backups 1.1. I made significant changes while merging this but I hope you'll agree that they're improvements:

- You used substring matching while I decided to go with shell pattern matching (using Python's [fnmatch](https://docs.python.org/2/library/fnmatch.html) module) because it allows for prefix, substring and suffix matching using the `*` wildcard. In general the use of fnmatch pattern elements allows for more flexible include/exclude matching (IMHO).
- I rewrote the inner loops to use Python's `any()` function and generator expressions because this is nicer to read (IMHO).
- You used `-c` as the short option for the include list (given that `-i` was already used for `ionice`) but I decided to use `-I` instead. I guess this is just a matter of taste :-).
- I added a test suite earlier today and decided to write tests for the include/exclude logic as well while merging this.

Rather than make a special folder for mms in /examples, I created a model-server folder, so any other model serving examples can be added there.

The first time a user goes to build something, you can;t just throw errors at him and refuse to build.  This discourages use of mxnet. I don;t know how many products I have abandoned because they seemed a pain to build and I wasn't willing to invest in fixing their build issues.

Very few people are going to have more than one version of cuda installed. And even if they do, their symbolic 'cuda' link will point to the preferred one. If they *want* to use a particular version, then they can set CUDA_TOOLSET to that version.

I think we should understand why it is failing on the machine in question and deal with that rather than choose the lowest-common-denonimator of HARD-CODING the version.

This should work in principle. I don't see anything obviously wrong, but you might want to try a few things:

1. use DEALER/ROUTER instead of REQ/REP. REQ/REP sockets are generally not encouraged for production use as they don't handle disconnects or other changes well.
2. try `tcp` while getting started. `ipc` *should* work, but it can hide bugs (e.g. I think multiple sockets can try to bind on the same ipc socket, which will cause problems such as lost messages by whichever socket bound first). I think there can also be problems if sockets connect before bind with ipc, or multiple sockets try to bind on the same ipc socket, where tcp will generally work fine or raise appropriate errors, rather than silently failing like ipc.

You can run an eventloop in the other thread and signal events that way, rather than run pollers yourself:

```python
from zmq.eventloop import IOLoop, ZMQStream

def handle_recv(msg):
    """I will be called whenever a message arrives on sock"""
    print(msg)

def handle_messages(socket):
    loop = IOLoop()
    loop.make_current()
    stream = ZMQStream(socket)
    stream.on_recv(handle_recv)
    loop.start()

ctx = zmq.Context()
sock = ctx.socket(zmq.SUB)
sock.connect('tcp://127.0.0.1:4000')
sock.subscribe(b'')
message_thread = Thread(target=handle_messages, args=(sock,))
message_thread.start()

```

Since pyzmq blocking methods release the GIL, it usually makes more sense to do these things with threads than processes. Do make sure to not use a socket simultaneously in multiple threads because zmq sockets are not threadsafe. If you put a thread on an IOLoop, then *all* operations on that socket should be called on that thread.

I'm sorry I've been letting this sit around so long. I've been wrestling with a few aspects of this PR and general direction:

1. If we land this or something like it, how do we maintain a list of TLDs going forward? Does that require us to do regular releases? Does that require us to do releases that are solely a TLD update? 
2. Are there security issues around TLDs that would be better served by separating maintenance of a list into a separate project?
3. This PR provides a command to update the TLD list, but we have no insight as to whether that operation is successful. It might be successful now, but what if it fails tomorrow? Will a user know that it's bad? Is there a way for a user to verify it? What's our responsibility for making sure it always works correctly?

I think requests has a similar situation where they used to have CA bundles in the requests package and then split that out into a separate project called certifi. Then certifi updates can come out independently of requests and it provides a nice API for people to use their own CA bundles.

I think treating this the same way is worth looking into. I want to take a look around to see what the current state of TLD-related Python libraries is and maybe we should use one of them.

I think the general idea from the discussion in #1622 was that an incremental approach would be good for getting real-time updates (especially given that this might be a bad thing for some users). That said, when I was looking into this about a month ago, I recall that it seemed to me like quite a bit of work was going to be involved to rearrange some of the parts of the backend to do this. This applies even more so to @QuentinBrosse's suggestions. So this means there are two alternative approaches:

1. Update the frontend using the existing backend (apart from minor tweaks here and there) before reworking the backend to better accommodate real-time updates on the frontend.
2. Rework the backend first, keeping in mind what the next generation frontend will be like.

The first approach seems more sensible to me. If I recall correctly, one of the complications with the existing backend was how the templates were being used (I forget the details, but I was originally going to do something with the templates to add rudimentary support for autorefresh and unfortunately I haven't had the time to devote to this). If the idea is to move to a frontend framework that does most of the job of the template engine, then this problem mostly goes away, which could minimize necessary backend changes.

@nchammas The original boto was, to a large extent, hand-written. That meant that customizations were often directly on the functions themselves. But since AWS has 60+ services, hand writing them all just doesn't scale. So boto3 is generated from api definitions that we get from upstream. We tried to migrate all the customizations over, but with such a large code base a few were missed. Now the customizations are much easier to see though (the vast majority are in [handlers.py](https://github.com/boto/botocore/blob/develop/botocore/handlers.py) in botocore and the rest are in boto3 service folders).

We have wrestled with the issue a bit more and have learnt some more about it.

Firstly, this is a strange (yet probably common) scenario: we are running code from the current directory of the source code of a package, but that package is installed (via pip) AS WELL.  This seems to confuse setuptools somewhat, because it picks up ./nspace as well as the installed site-packages version of nspace.  (./nspace only contains two, whereas the version in site-packages contains three as well.)

Secondly, we realised that this is related to setuptools' handling of PEP420 implicit namespaces.

According to PEP420, namespace packages should not have __init__.py files at all. We're on Python 3.5 with this issue, thus, according to PEP420, our namespace packages should not have __init__.py files. This example had __init__.py files that contained calls to .declare_namespace(). If we remove the relevant __init__.py files, the problem goes away.

What causes the issue is: 

There are two versions of the nspace package:
  ./nspace (which is part of the source code of nspace-two)
and
 ....site-packages/nspace

When importing nspace.three, somehow the ./nspace is found first, and inside it it finds an __init__.py. According to PEP420 then, if a package has an __init__.py in it the package is treated as a normal package - NOT a namespace package. 

I am not sure how one would handle this scenario. Our code base runs on Python 2.7 as well and I'm not sure if one still needs __init__.py files in that case, since PEP420 is Python >= 3.3. Perhaps a dependency on new enough setuptools would solve it?

On the other hand, the situation where you run, eg "python ./setup.py" in the source code tree of a package which also happens to be installed in site-packages is kindof weird. Yet, I think it often happens. For example, when people run tests against installed packages: python ./setup.py test.

Thanks for the feedback - it's super helpful! Responses below:

1. The default value of 0 simply mirrors the previous behavior. If there's enough call for changing that default, I think it will make sense to do so.
2. Yes, I'm very interested in avoiding breaking changes, since pykafka is used as a core infrastructure piece in many systems. I also know that at least for workloads like @parsely's, the producer queues are in practice *never* empty, so this pull request is a complete noop for it and similar workloads. Still, as mentioned above, I'm open to changing the default if I hear enough community desire for that change. It's not clear to me how often pykafka is used for workloads similar to Parse.ly's, and how often it's used in situations where the queue can fully empty.
3. I don't know where a longer delay has been introduced. By default, the timing behavior of queue flushes should exactly match previous behavior. When the queue empty timeout is adjusted, flush time will increase only when the queue was previously completely empty. Furthermore, it increases in a predictable way relative to the user-defined setting. I don't think there needs to be regressoin documentation beyond what's added in this PR, but please tell me if you think I'm overlooking something.

The syntax is a bit odd, since `.`s generally don't denote inheritance in Python (might as well return a list of classes in that case). More importantly though, thanks to multiple inheritance, it isn't necessarily a linear ancestry, but rather a tree as any class can potentially have more than one parent. We could, pass this tree along in the JSON but it just seems... messy. Hence this is why we settled on duck typing in this case.

@zpincus I have not helped things by going off on my layout efficiency tangent, but I think you have a misunderstanding of what xy/rc mean in the context of skimage. The point is not actually that we want to be prescriptive about axis order. (Actually if you look above I voted for the nomenclature with the *least* referential baggage, "M, N, P". Perhaps you want to cast your vote for that also? 😉)

The point is that we ~~were not~~ **are not** *consistent* in our axis convention. So, the `transform` module uses what we are calling here "xy" coordinates, that is, the first axis is the horizontal and the second is vertical, with the origin on the top-left, *which doesn't match NumPy indexing* any way you slice it. Before I started on this quest, this type of inconsistent coordinate use was scattered all over the library. Now I think only a few spots remain.

`ndimage` is wonderful, but certainly if you give it a Fortran-ordered array but use *horizontal first* axis coordinates, you will not get the results you think. Even when indexing a F-ordered array, in NumPy or ndimage, the first coordinate is the rows, and the second coordinate is the columns, and the only thing that changes is the optimal order of iteration.

The xy/rc distinction is necessary, therefore, so that at least people who are using the library one way or another know which functions require them to swap axes around. Perhaps in the far future we can redo all our docs so that we stop referring to "rows and columns" and instead we go for "axis 0 and axis 1". But is that really much better?

Concerning the most recent Travis failure, it seems to be an issue with `print` being a function in Python 3. > My only concern is about redundancy in the commit message, are we going to write closes #nnn and also fixes #nnn? 

Nope, usually the addition of leading label should be sufficient.  

Here is one simple example:

- commit message old style:

```
commit b957a8a21d57c0801538bb93ee05f7bd7d04e5e3
Author: Tibor Simko <tibor.simko@cern.ch>
Date:   Wed Jan 28 10:23:16 2015 +0100

    BibIndex: fix new-old record incremental indexing
    
    * Fixes incremental indexing leak for "new old" records, i.e. new
      records that were uploaded as if they were old, via some 005 value
      from the past.  (closes #2693)
    
    * Also, amends `find_affected_records_for_index()` default parameter
      values from mutable empty lists to immutable `None` in order to
      prevent potential mishaps.
    
    Signed-off-by: Tibor Simko <tibor.simko@cern.ch>`
```

- commit message new style

```
commit b957a8a21d57c0801538bb93ee05f7bd7d04e5e3
Author: Tibor Simko <tibor.simko@cern.ch>
Date:   Wed Jan 28 10:23:16 2015 +0100

    BibIndex: fix new-old record incremental indexing
    
    * FIX Fixes incremental indexing leak for "new old" records, i.e. new
      records that were uploaded as if they were old, via some 005 value
      from the past.  (closes #2693)
    
    * Also, amends `find_affected_records_for_index()` default parameter
      values from mutable empty lists to immutable `None` in order to
      prevent potential mishaps.
    
    Signed-off-by: Tibor Simko <tibor.simko@cern.ch>
```

This commit contains headline and two paragraphs.  The headline would be ignored, the first paragraph would be used inside a "Bug fixed" section of the release notes, and the second paragraph would be ignored as well.  (Or, better said, only visible to developers using git logs.)

***

To take a slightly more complex example, consider the following commit message:

```
commit 13d3840146bdb9be52885ec91af357a017ba17be
Author: Nikolaos Kasioumis <nikolaos.kasioumis@cern.ch>
Date:   Tue May 27 21:56:05 2014 +0200

    WebSearch: search results pattern limit fix
    
    * Fixes pattern-limit style of queries (`/search?subject=physics`)
      by modifying `prs_apply_search_limits` so that the `results_final`
      dictionary is updated, rather than re-defined locally.
      (closes #1750) (PR #1751)
    
    Signed-off-by: Nikolaos Kasioumis <nikolaos.kasioumis@cern.ch>
    Co-authored-by: Ludmila Marian <ludmila.marian@gmail.com>
    Tested-by: Tibor Simko <tibor.simko@cern.ch>`
```

In the new commit log style, one would want to reword the message to something user-oriented and something developer-oriented, for example:

```
commit 13d3840146bdb9be52885ec91af357a017ba17be
Author: Nikolaos Kasioumis <nikolaos.kasioumis@cern.ch>
Date:   Tue May 27 21:56:05 2014 +0200

    WebSearch: search results pattern limit fix
    
    * FIX Takes properly into account the search pattern limit queries
      (for example `/search?subject=physics`) that might have been 
      passed from the Advanced Search interface.
      (closes #1750) (PR #1751)

    * This is done by modifying `prs_apply_search_limits` so that 
       `results_final`  dictionary is updated, rather than re-defined locally.
    
    Signed-off-by: Nikolaos Kasioumis <nikolaos.kasioumis@cern.ch>
    Co-authored-by: Ludmila Marian <ludmila.marian@gmail.com>
    Tested-by: Tibor Simko <tibor.simko@cern.ch>
```

A custom constructor means at least these things:

1. The new pickles can't be read by older releases. This can be annoying for moving databases between environments. It may or may not be a show stopper, I don't know.
2. The new constructor function effectively becomes part of the API of the package and can't be removed without breaking pickles.
2. The higher pickle protocol optimizations don't kick in, so the pickles are slightly larger/slower than they would be using those protocols.
3. The pickle formats produced by C and Python implementations would diverge unless someone wants to go in and mess with the C code (I don't). They're different now, so that's not a huge deal, but it would be nice if they were the same (I was *this* close to that, until I tested on Python 3).

```
class BlacklistTokenAuthenticationTest(TestCase):
    urls = 'tests.test_authentication'

    def setUp(self):
        self.csrf_client = APIClient(enforce_csrf_checks=True)
        self.username = 'jpueblo'
        self.email = 'jpueblo@example.com'
        self.user = User.objects.create_user(self.username, self.email)
        
        api_settings.JWT_ENABLE_BLACKLIST = True

    def test_post_blacklisted_token_failing_jwt_auth(self):
        """
        Ensure POSTing over JWT auth with blacklisted token fails
        """
        payload = utils.jwt_payload_handler(self.user)
        token = utils.jwt_encode_handler(payload)

        # Create blacklist token which effectively blacklists the token.
        JWTBlackListToken.objects.create(jti=payload.get('jti'),
                                         created=now(), expires=now()) 

        auth = 'JWT {0}'.format(token)
        response = self.csrf_client.post(
            '/jwt/', {'example': 'example'},
            HTTP_AUTHORIZATION=auth, format='json')

        msg = 'Token is blacklisted.'

        self.assertEqual(response.data['detail'], msg)
        self.assertEqual(response.status_code, status.HTTP_401_UNAUTHORIZED)
        self.assertEqual(response['WWW-Authenticate'], 'JWT realm="api"')
```

> Sorry I'm kind of using this issue as a feedback post at this point.

Not a problem.

> I personally feel the zero-arity function shouldn't get called even when --help is used.

Yes, largely I agree. Currently I'm leaning toward having --help behave the same as '-- --help' (e.g. preventing the final fn/class from being called/instantiated) except in the case where the fn accepts a help argument (either explicitly or via **kwargs). I want to as much as possible allow Fire to work on any Python component at all.

> Why does a callable class not get called?

This is because we currently only look for argspecs [on classes and routines](https://github.com/google/python-fire/blob/master/fire/core.py#L319), but not on other objects (in your example it's the instantiated object that is callable, not the class itself).
I am certainly open to changing this behavior. It's a nontrivial change and could introduce ambiguous commands, and so will require careful thought.

Oh right. This is new. In EVM it may not be used concurrently as the generate_testcase is done "offline" at the main thread/proc. 

In legacy manticore testcases are generated concurrently "online" so the files and file ids and redis ids are generated concurrently.

You can use DyNet's multi-processing functionality, which may solve your problem (http://dynet.readthedocs.io/en/latest/multiprocessing.html). Using multiple threads within a single is not supported at the moment. I've heard that there are people interested in this and potentially working on it, so if things go well we might be able to push a thread-safe version.

Yes, it's OK to adopt code form other Apache licensed projects to DyNet. If the license is something other than Apache, we'll need to be careful.

Hi,

I was able to solve the problem by editing the pympler source.  In asizeof.py, edit the `flat()` method to read as follows:

`def flat(self, obj, mask=0):
    '''Return the aligned flat size.
    '''
    s = self.base
    if isinstance(obj,np.ndarray):
        s = obj.nbytes
    else:
        if self.leng and self.item > 0:  # include items
            s += self.leng(obj) * self.item
        if _getsizeof:  # _getsizeof prevails
            s = _getsizeof(obj, s)
    if mask:  # align
        s = (s + mask) & ~mask
    return s`

Worked for me, but I am by no means an expert and this may not be a general solution.

@clowwindy good point re: decryption. You're right that if we're aiming for indistinguishability against adversaries determined enough to run different decryption attempts on suspicious traffic (that's expensive because there probably are tens, if not hundreds, possible other "suspect sources" besides shadowsocks, but GFW in general is expensive, so there :) ) your two-password proposal is probably one of most simple and efficient solutions. So, let's try to make it a little more efficient still. We now have two pre-shared secrets : user_password and server_password (it would be prudent to have them different, so if user tries to have "user_password == server_password", we should at least give a fair warning and advice to make them at least slightly different) Because user-generated passwords are usually rather crappy, we should probably derive keys from them. Since this is a one-time affair (reasonable implementations of both client and server should store the derived keys long-term after being supplied with relevant user_password and server_password) we should probably use a really modern secure hash here. (The GFW people could still try creating a giant rainbow table of all likely user_password/username/server_password combinations, but at this point the user has to "merely" use good, long, high-entropy passwords to thwart such shenanigans. Since user_password and server_password have to be entered just once during client and server configuration, that's not too much hassle, in my opinion) Thus ukey = SHA3(user_password) servkey = SHA3(server_password) SHA3 is a secure and fast hash, good enough for deriving our keys (it's slower than MD5 :-) , but we only do this once when configuring the client and when configuring the server) To recap: Each user has only one ukey. Each server has only one servkey. they are generated from user_password and server_password respectively during setup, and stored. Now... Let's make the entire message thing in the following manner IV1 + ENCSITKey(ver + userhash) + ENCUITKey(addr + data) SITKey = MD5(IV1+servkey) UITKey = MD5(IV1+ukey) ENC = whatever cipher configured during server setup (RC4-MD5 is probably the favorite for situations where speed > security, though, maybe make it default setting) Now, when our shadowsocks server recieves this entire thing, it reconstructs the SITKey by taking the IV1, concatenating it with servkey (which was generated from server_password during server setup) and decrypts "ver" and "userhash" if "ver" is sane, and userhash belongs to a known user, the server proceeds to reconstruct UITKey ( by taking the IV1, concatenating it with user's respective ukey, which server knows) and then server decrypts (addr + data). If "ver" is wrong or userhash is not one of known users, server drops the entire thing. The GFW cannot decrypt (ver + userhash) in reasonable time without knowing server_password (and thus servkey). Use of SHA3 when generating servkey and ukey ensures that we don't run into issues associated with low entropy of user-supplied passwords. Also, do note that we need only one pseudorandom nonce as IV here (IV1), which makes the message slightly smaller and thus slightly more efficient. So, your opinion on this version? P.S.: Very good points with regards to failings of ScrambleSuit and the like. A lot of research in this area is really a bit too academic. Besides, stuff like SS or Meek or TOR really devour the battery on mobile, and works poorly on 3G. Shadowsocks is currently my favorite precisely because it works wonders on my mobile (though the android client does seem to devour battery way too fast on lolipop, which I fixed by downgrading to KitKat)

#!/usr/bin/env python
import argparse
import configparser
import sys
import os
import boto.cloudformation
import yaml
from random import randint
from troposphere import GetAtt, Join, Output
from troposphere import Parameter, Ref, Template
from troposphere import route53
from troposphere import waf
from troposphere.waf import FieldToMatch
from troposphere.waf import SqlInjectionMatchTuples

```
class Waf(object):

    def __init__(self, accountName, environment):
        self.account_name = accountName
        self.environment = environment
        self.label=self.account_name+'-'+self.environment
        self.stackName=self.environment+"-waf"

    def get_connection_cloudformation(self):
        if self.environment is not None:
            config = configparser.ConfigParser()
            try:
              config.read(os.path.expanduser('~')+'/.aws/credentials')
            except Exception as error:
                  print "config file doesn't exist"
                  print error
                  exit(1)
        aws_access_key = config[self.label]['aws_access_key_id']
        aws_secret_key = config[self.label]['aws_secret_access_key']
        region = config[self.label]['region']

        conn=boto.cloudformation.connect_to_region(region, aws_access_key_id=aws_access_key, aws_secret_access_key=aws_secret_key)
        return conn

    def create_template_waf(self):
        t = Template()
        t.add_description(
            "AWS WAF Deployment"
            )

        wafSqliMatchSetName = b'["sqlimatchset"]',
        wafFieldToMatch_sqli = FieldToMatch(Type=b'Ranodm')
        wafSqliMatchSetTuples = SqlInjectionMatchTuples(FieldToMatch=wafFieldToMatch_sqli)

        wafSqliMatchSet = waf.SqlInjectionMatchSet(Name=wafSqliMatchSetName,
                                                   SqlInjectionMatchTuples=wafSqliMatchSetTuples)

        wafRuleName_sqli = [b'sqli']
        wafRuleMetricName_sqli = [b'sqlinjectioncount']
        wafRule_sqli = waf.Rule(
            Name = wafRuleName_sqli,
            MetricName = wafRuleMetricName_sqli,
            )


        wafDefaultAcl_sqli = "Allow"
        wafMetricName_sqli = "SqlInjWebACL"
        wafName_sqli = "Web ACL to block SQL injection in the query string"
        Waf = waf.WebACL(
            DefaultAction = wafDefaultAcl_sqli,
            MetricName = wafMetricName_sqli,
            Name = wafName_sqli,
            Rules = [ wafRule_sqli ],
            )
        t.add_resource(Waf)
        template = t.to_json()

        # 1.	Generate the SQL Injection Condition
        # 2.	Add a Condition Match to the Condition to analyze the Query String in the URL.
        # 3.	Create the Rule
        # 4.	Add the Condition to the Rule
        # 5.	Create the Web ACL
        # 6. 	Add the Rule to the Web ACL


        print template
        conn = self.get_connection_cloudformation()
        exist = self.exist_cloud_formation(conn)
        print exist
        if exist:
            try:
                result = conn.update_stack(self.stackName, template_body=t.to_json(), capabilities=['CAPABILITY_IAM'])
            except boto.exception.BotoServerError as e:
                if e.message != "No updates are to be performed.":
                    print("[ERROR] " + e.message)
                    exit(1)
                else:
                    print(e.message)
                    exit(0)

        else:
                result = conn.create_stack(self.stackName, template_body=t.to_json(), capabilities=['CAPABILITY_IAM'])
        print result

    def exist_cloud_formation(self, cf):
        stack_summaries = cf.describe_stacks()
        if len(stack_summaries) > 0:
            for StackSummary in stack_summaries:
                if StackSummary.stack_name == self.stackName:
                    exist = True
                    break
                else:
                    exist = False

        return exist
```

Well, it's not immediately apparent to me what's going wrong, but two things I notice:

1. `document.add_table()` is going to put a table at the end of the document. That's what it does. So minimally, any new tables aren't going to be in the middle of the document. There's no reason it should overwrite anything however. Same thing with `.add_heading()`; this will put a new paragraph at the end. So maybe your last page is just getting moved to the middle, or wherever you first called one of these.

2. `_Cell.text` is destructive to whatever text was there before (and also to its run-level formatting). That's the only thing I can see that would overwrite anything.

If you want to do more diagnosis, I recommend paring down this example to the absolute minimum it takes to reproduce the behavior, so only one iteration of `leaf_n` for a start and only one cell write, maybe one row if that turns out to be necessary. This will narrow things down a lot.

Also, I think you're going to want to learn how to inspect the XML in the `document.xml` part using `opc-diag`. This is what python-pptx operates on and what determines document behavior. So it would definitely be the next step I would proceed to after getting the code pared down to a minimum.

@jbankston, the item values are the path at which the details of the resources may be found. So for example, for IAM users, it will look like services.iam.users.<USER_ID>, where <USER_ID> is the ID set by AWS. 

In the VPC / Compute world, it would look like  services.<SERVICE_NAME>.regions.<REGION_NAME>.vpcs.<VPC_ID>.<RESOURCE_TYPE>.<RESOURCE_ID>. The resource ID is the ID set by AWS when available (sg-XXX, vpc-XXX, etc...). If no ID is set/exposed by the AWS service, it will be the name of the resource if the value cannot contain dots/periods. Last resort would be a md5 of the resource name.

@chrishales709 I've been able to implement IOM's reconnect functionality in my saspy java code, for a simple case - to prototype it with. There's still more cases to handle in there, and that brings me to the next set of issues/questions. 

What exactly happens when you 'close the lid' on your laptop? Sleep, Hibernate? Is the network adapter disabled? All kinds of possibilities here, and I believe there's going to be more than just this part that I've  got so far with IOM.

There's a few things going on that all have to be accounted for. 
1) saspy python process
2) saspy java process
3) sockets between these two processes (loopback adapter local to the laptop)
4) IOM client in the java process
5) network connection between java client and workspace server (remote network connection)

So, this part addresses a disruption of number 5 above. it's implemented in number 4 above.
Now, I need to know what's going on with the first 3 for your particular case. Are both python/java processes (1,2)  still up and running? Is the socket between the two (3) still functioning? Given you got the error: "No SAS process attached. SAS process has terminated unexpectedly."
that means to me that the java process (2) is gone as well as the socket (3) between it and python (1).
This would happen if only 5 goes away (2 will then go away and then 3). It would also happen if something happend to 3 as well.

This is where I need to understand what is happening on your machine when you close the lid. If only the remote network connection (5) is lost and 1,2,3 are still able to function (Sleep mode case; I believe) then I think we're close. I still have work to do to get this fully implemented, but this part is working.

If the network adapter is being dissabled, and the loopback (internal) connection is lost too, then that will take out 3, which will cause 2 to terminate and I'll have to invent a new protocol for reconnecting saspy's python and java processes. That would involve catching the socket failures in both processes and having some kind of reconnection scheme. That's certainly a lot more work, but I imagine I can come up with something if I have to.

So, can you identify what's really happening when you close your lid? The code below can be used to test if the loopback adapter is disconnecting, without having to try to figure it out on your own.
If it is and you can configure your network adapter to stay alive even with the lid closed (if it's not currently), that would make things much better too. 

Here's a standalone pair of programs you can run, then close the lid (do whatever it is) and then see if they are still running after you get back up and running again. I just run these from a command prompt (two different ones), though you could run them in jupyter too (two different notebooks).

Submit this in one python process first. It will print out the port the second one needs.
```
import socket as socks
import time
from time import sleep

sockin  = socks.socket()
sockin.bind(("",0))
port = sockin.getsockname()[1]
sockin.listen(1)
print("port to connect to is "+str(port))

sock = sockin.accept()

while True:
  ti  = time.localtime()
  tis = str(ti.tm_hour)+':'+str(ti.tm_min)+':'+str(ti.tm_sec)
  sock[0].send(tis.encode())
  print(sock[0].recv(256).decode())
  sleep(5)
```

```

If these two processes are still interacting after your back from the lid close, then we should be in good shape to address only losing 5.

Ok.  So I've got a v1.12.2 cluster up and running right now.  I'm using proxy proto, a valid wildcard cert for my domain, and `enforceHTTPS=true`.

I am not encountering any problem at all with this configuration.

Where I _do_ encounter a problem is when I go back to @Crispy1975's original configuration.  If I attempt to terminate SSL at the load balancer with proxy protocol enabled (on the ELB itself and on the router end) then I do have problems, although they look more like errors to me than a redirect loop.

At any rate... this experimentation has reduced my belief that there is an actual problem here.  When a load balancer terminates an HTTPS request and initiates an HTTP request to the backend, all HTTP headers should be set appropriately, including `X-Forwarded-For`, and afaik, that should negate any practical need for using proxy protocol to begin with.  (That is to say, proxy protocol is most needed wherever HTTP is _not_ spoken and raw TCP traffic is being piped through to the router.)

The conclusion I am drawing is this:

* Terminating SSL at the ELB is (of course) mutually exclusive with using TCP listeners on 80 and 443
* PROXY protocol is only useful when using TCP listeners
* Terminating SSL at the ELB and using PROXY protocol are therefor mutually exclusive

Ultimately, I do believe that _not_ terminating SSL at the ELB is the more common use case.  Since that means using TCP listeners, that means also leaving PROXY protocol enabled by default since that will allow the router to learn the end user's IP in the absence of an `X-Forwarded-For` HTTP header that the ELB would _not_ have set in such a case.

I would suggest that disabling PROXY protocol by default undermines this most common use case.

Now my inclination toward humility asserts itself-- it's possible I have this entirely wrong and if that is the case, I am eager for whatever I am missing to be pointed out.  :)

@bd4 After spending some time fiddling with things and thinking about how to swing all of this, I'm starting to come around to the idea of an object oriented model like the one we discarded in #41 (discussing `no_auth_header`).

Here's the problem that I see: we have at least 4 different ways of generating Authorization headers, at least 3 of which should have the same interface for `BaseClient`.
- No header
- Basic Auth (client credentials)
- Access Token (but no Refresh Token)
- Access Token + Refresh Token to handle expiration (also covers Refresh Token sans Access Token)

Now, not having an Authorization header at all is kinda weird -- I'm not sure that _has_ to be handled the same way as the others.

I did some scribbles this morning, and I was thinking of this:

1. Every `BaseClient` may have a `GlobusAuthorizer` instance attached to it.
2. `BaseClient._request` uses `BaseClient.authorizer.get_authorization_header()` to get an Authorization header
3. Upon seeing a 401, `BaseClient._request` will try `BaseClient.authorizer.missing_authentication_handler(...)` which returns either `GlobusAuthorizer.HANDLER_CODE_ABORT` or `GlobusAuthorizer.HANDLER_CODE_RETRY` and retry once (and only once!) unless we got `no_authorization_retry=True` as an arg to `_request()`

I'm not sure what parameters would be useful/necessary for `missing_authentication_handler`, but I think we should specify that it must accept arbitrary args/kwargs in case someone else wants to implement one and we change the interface a bit in the future.

This covers the various needs nicely because `get_authorization_header()` for each of the auth types has a clear and obvious meaning.
For Basic Auth and a raw Access Token, format a string, and for Access Tokens + Refresh Tokens, you can do a lot of logic there: check `expires_in` if you have it (tracked in the authorizer itself), try the Access Token format string, and handle retries by doing a refresh.

---

Now, pushing this thinking further (don't make fun of me like I'm a Java zealot, this just seems like the right pattern!), we need to talk about the `GlobusAuthorizer` Factories & Builders.

Class constructors are fine for `NullAuthorizer` (if we go that route), `BasicAuthorizer`, and `AccessTokenAuthorizer`.
But a lot of params go into `RefreshTokenAuthorizer` -- and in some cases you've *just* gotten the tokens that you're about to use...

Should `GlobusOAuthTokenResponse` have a method for this?
I'm thinking `GlobusOAuthTokenResponse.build_authorizer(<resource_server_name>)` which does the following:
- If there are only access tokens, gets the correct token for the given resource server and make an `AccessTokenAuthorizer`
- If there are refresh tokens, get the all of the available info (`expires_in` plus the tokens, really) and make a `RefreshTokenAuthorizer`

Final usage would look something like this, to the outside world:

```
ac = AuthClient(client_id=..., client_secret=...) # implicitly creates and attaches a BasicAuthorizer
# do OAuth flow to get token_response
...
tc = TransferClient(authorizer=token_response.build_authorizer('transfer.api.globus.org'))
```

or you could also do

```
tc = TransferClient(token=...) # implicitly makes an AccessTokenAuthorizer
```

or even

```
# omits access token and expires_in -- they'll be found on the initial, automatic refresh
tc = TransferClient(authorizer=RefreshTokenAuthorizer(client_id=..., refresh_token=...))
```

which really is the same as

```
tc = TransferClient(
    authorizer=RefreshTokenAuthorizer(auth_client=AuthClient(client_id=...), refresh_token=...)
```

but we probably don't actually expose that last one in the `RefreshTokenAuthorizer`'s available constructor parameters.

:weary:
There's so much going on here...
I don't want to balloon the SDK complexity, but I think the `GlobusAuthorizer` pattern might be the least-bad path forward.

Thanks for the quick update! The `prefer_unicode` thing is a rather new addition, and currently it only spans parameters, result sets, and the query text itself. That means that indeed `prefer_unicode` will not solve your problem. While this issue is not fully resolved, you could try the following things:

* If you have a choice of ANSI and Unicode drivers, try the ANSI driver. For ANSI drivers, try to set the session encoding (or similar option) to UTF-8. If there is no session locale option, you can try setting the `LOCALE` environment variable to an UTF-8 locale.
* Use a different query that maps the column names in your view to ASCII names.
* Try to retrieve data via another view that maps the cyrillic column names of your original view to ASCII names.
* Use a different query (`SELECT cyrillic_name AS ascii_name, ...`) that maps the column names in your view to ASCII names.

Try to combine options 2 and 3 with `prefer_unicode`.

On another note, I would suggest not to use `pd.read_sql()`, because the [documentation](http://pandas.pydata.org/pandas-docs/version/0.20/generated/pandas.read_sql.html) claims that this only supports SQLite if you do not use SQLAlchemy at the same time. The preferred and likely _way_ faster method should be this:
```
import turbodbc
import pandas as pd

connection = turbodbc.connect(dsn='my DSN')
cursor = connection.cursor()
cursor.execute('SELECT * FROM table')
df = pd.Dataframe(data=cursor.fetchallnumpy())
```

Thanks for starting the discussion here, @jakirkham.  For [Pyodide](https://github.com/iodide-project/pyodide), a Mozilla project to bring the Python scientific stack to WebAssembly, we have been looking at using conda as the build infrastructure -- essentially adding wasm as a new target alongside Linux, Mac and Windows.  It seems there's a lot of commonality we could reuse there.  But, given time constraints, we haven't done much beyond discussion and hand waving.  If there was someone with more conda-build experience who was interested in helping out, that might move things forward more quickly.

> Of course Emscripten can be used to do this, but packaging Emscripten is hard. Not to mention it relies on things like LLVM that are also hard to package. 

I don't know enough about conda-build yet.  Is it critical that conda-build itself builds the build dependencies, or would it be enough to use an `emsdk` already present?  Wouldn't this be somewhat analogous to the Windows platform, where the compilers can't be built but are just "provided"?  It seems like, at a minimum, we could create a conda package that is a thin wrapper around emsdk.

As a first pass, I don't think WebAssembly builds need to be self-hosting (i.e. built from within node or a browser).  Having the compiler toolchain run in a native environment supports the majority use case of creating packages for use later in WebAssembly.

> Also there are a few other toolchain choices, so what is the right one to pick?

I'm not aware of another toolchain that addresses the problem of porting legacy C and C++ to WebAssembly as completely as emscripten.  Most of the others are only appropriate if you were starting today and targetting WebAssembly, and don't need a filesystem or the cstdlib, etc.  Will we need to support both approaches eventually?  Probably yes, but the really effective thing now will be to get legacy tools built.

> Does everything get "statically linked" into one huge blob or is there a good strategy for declaring dependencies?

There are sort of two separate issues here: 

- Dynamic linking is supported, and `dlopen` works basically as you would expect when using emscripten, so it is possible to load `.so` files (including Python extensions) at runtime.  There are some problems to solve on Chrome given its requirement for asynchronous compilation, but hopefully those can get addressed.

- You don't have to deliver the file contents in one big file.  Emscripten allows filesystems to be overlayed, so you can, as we do in Pyodide, load the core Python standard library, and then optionally load Numpy into site-packages as a separate network request.  You could, alternatively, do this on a per-file-basis with the obvious tradeoffs.

- A good strategy to declare dependencies would probably be to model it after conda's dependency solver.  We might want to port the important bits of the conda client to Javascript or Rust/C/Wasm to make this work.  (Pyodide, atm, only makes available one version of any given package, so dependency resolution is very simple, but it's just done on the client, and then the filesystem overlays are loaded accordingly).

@joelkim any objections to the PR as I've refactored it?

> conda doesn't just try to satisfy deps, it also tries to get the latest version of all deps that satisfy reqs, even if that means updating an otherwise perfectly functional set of pkgs.

Oh, I can understand why this would be a bad thing, but it is also is a false choice There is a third option, which we have implemented in the update. Here's the behavior as we have implemented:

- `--update-deps`, the default: identify the _minimum_ set of dependency changes to the current environment to allow the explicitly requested packages to be updated.
- `--no-update-deps`: no currently installed packages outside of the install list are to be updated. This will, of course, sometimes limit the amount of updating that occurs.

What we are *not* doing is attempting to retrieve the latest versions of all dependencies. The goal is minimum disruption of the environment to achieve the user's request, _but in a compliant manner_. If you want dependencies to be fully updated, you have two choices: 1) put them in the install list, and 2) `conda update --all` (ugh!)

> no distinction between "must have" and "nice to have" packages, so dep list can grow to be longer than is necessary

I absolutely agree. This is entirely doable with the new solver architecture. In fact I've been advocating for keeping track of packages that were _explicitly requested_ versus ones that were installed solely because of dependency requirements. Then if a combination of updates happens to render a dependency unnecessary, it can be removed (with appropriate warning/confirmation).

> check satisfiability of all env pkg deps at install time
> check satisfiability of all env pkg deps after install, and warn.

These are good ideas. The current update does a better job of ensuring satisfiability of the current environment but I don't think it pulls in every package---just the ones affected by the current operation. 

> But if it is not set, then the unit of atomic pkg consistency is a single command. Previous commands are not considered.

If you are suggesting that we allow `conda install` or `conda update` to cause an unsatisfiable environment without the use of a `--force` command, then I reject this totally. If we need to do more adjustments to the current environment than the user was expecting, it will be evident by the longer-than-expected list of changes conda is making. The user is free to hit "N" and investigate further why this is happening. And we could certainly do a better job of offering diagnostics as to why those additional steps are necessary.

I understand the temptation to _claim_ a set of packages is "perfectly functional" even if it violates its own dependency requirements. And of course the claim might even be true. But _the developers of the packages themselves_ are not making that claim, and we should not silently override their judgement. If you want to keep a broken set of requirements in place, you should be enabled to do so. But you should be fully aware that is what is happening.

Here's what I've found. 

Environments created with older versions of `conda` don't have the `url` field included in their `conda-meta` metadata. This prevents `conda` from determining what channel it came from, and therefore whether the packages in the package cache represent the proper version of the package to clone.

The good news is this: environments created with `conda 4.0.8` or later include this `url` field, which allows cloning to proceed normally. So if you were to re-create this environment again using the later version of conda, all would be well. Furthermore, `conda 4.1.x` will do one better, and support fully offline cloning as long as all of the correct packages are in the cache. Previous versions of conda would still try and query Anaconda Cloud.

The bad news: we can't clone these older environments with 4.1, it seems, because we don't know where the packages came from. This can be fixed, by basically searching the users' channel lists in priority order for the requested package dist, and accepting the first match. But let me suggest we do this in 4.1.1.

This is a place where `curator_cli` does not normally check for an empty list, unfortunately.  What's happening is that you are processing your filters, and they are checking for an empty list condition, which is passing, as you have indices after your filters.  What's happening next, however, is that the action itself is checking for closed indices.  And all of the indices you're attempting to close are already closed, so the _action_ is self filtering the actionable list to remove indices already closed.  When it discovered that your list only had closed indices, the list was empty, and it throws the exception there.

While I can (and probably will) add a special check for "closed" indices before running actions (for actions which automatically do this), to avoid this behavior, it will not be added soon.  A great workaround would be for you to add `"filtertype": "closed"` to your filter list.  That will perform the step the action is taking in the filter section, and will result in the `0` exit code you seek.

I understand the desire to make AAD concepts more approachable, but we will have the opposite effect if we try to paper over them with yet another set of non-standard terms (ClientID + Password is not a common convention, and I actually really don't care for `login` using `username`/`password` with `--service-principal`.) 

`client_id` and `client_secret` are common terms in this area. The difference is that AAD separates the "app" from the "service principal" and previously required users to understand both in order to actually use any of these automated scenarios. I don't think there's really a problem in outputting them as Client ID and Client Secret.

If we really must output these helpful messages during the `create-for-rbac` command, then I would agree with @derekbekoe that they should be to stderr.

I guess, let me put it this way. This makes me question how much we really need this helpful text... Do we really think we're going to have users that go:

1. I know I want an automated login.
2. I know I need to `az ad sp create-for-rbac`
3. But somehow I don't know that I can login with these creds.

If your username is "AzureStackAdmin" (which is what it looks like from your screenshot) then that is why it fails.  You can supply `--admin-username` and it should work fine. We also could arguably take the lowercase version of the current user, which would probably also be fine. It's not too adventurous but would save people some headache. @derekbekoe thoughts?

Setuptools is based on buildstep, which technically could be re-used by further build to save some processing time (like Maven for Java or Makefile for C).
For this to work, this assumes that all the plugin you use respect each build step as a black box. This is not always the case unfortunately, and there is issues opened about this, which has various consequences difficult to predict. Example:
https://github.com/pypa/setuptools/issues/1064

Best approach is to build sdist and wheel with a full cleaning in between, to be sure setuptools do not try to re-use something corrupting by a previous plugin. In pratical terms, building wheel before sdist is usually enough, since sdist will invalidate wheel buildsteps and rebuild from scratch (roughly)

ws_url is a very useful option for us at [Instabase](https://www.instabase.com). 

We ran into a problem with GCloud load balancers. There are two types of them: Network and HTTP. (1) Network LB supports websockets but doesn't forward client ip to upstream, (2) HTTP LB forwards client ip to upstream but doesn't support websockets. 

We partitioned our traffic wherein websocket traffic went to a network lb and the rest went to a http lb. ws_url option saved the day for us and allowed us to keep our infrastructure stack fairly simple.

It sounds like you're trying an import in two different installations of Python, or two different environments. Check `sys.executable` to see which Python and environment you're running in, and `sys.path` to see where it looks to import modules.

Python packages must be installed separately for each copy of Python you use, and if you are using virtualenvs or conda envs, packages must be installed into each environment where you need them. Either the package is not installed in one, or a different version of the package is installed.

To install packages to a particular Python installation or environment using pip, you can run it like this:

```shell
path/to/python -m pip install ...
# Replace path/to/python with the path of the Python executable (sys.executable)
```

If you are using conda environments, you can install packages from conda like this:

```shell
source activate myenv  # On Windows, just 'activate myenv' (no 'source')
conda install ...
```

Documenting an in-person design discussion with @tacaswell:

Goals:
* Create a path to fully removing `doct` and just representing documents as plain dictionary objects.
* Do not break dot access in this release. That is, continue to support `event.data` as a synonym for `event['data']`... at least for now.

Proposed path:

1. Remove the special `_name` key from doct, so that the `Doct` object becomes nothing more than a dict subclass that supports `__getattr__` (dot access) as a synonym for `__getitem__` (square bracket lookup). Removing `_name` is an API change, but in practice it is not expected to break any user code.
2. Make all internal Broker components (mds and fs variants) represent documents as plain dict objects internally, thus saving on performance.
3. Wherever Broker returns document to the user, as in `header.stream()`, pass the document through a new `Broker.prepare_hook(name, doc)` method on the way out. By default, `prepare_hook` will convert the dict into a `Doct`, thus maintaining API continuity for the user. But the user can change `prepare_hook` to, for example, make a deep copy instead, and return a plain dict.
4. Make `Doct.__getattr__` warn that dot access may be removed in a future release. See how much push-back we get about removing dot access.
5. If removing doct entirely (and breaking dot access) still feel like a good idea, change the default behavior of `prepare_hook` in a future release.

{

Then I ran `csc` on it, specifying the two files in different order and got:

```
PS C:\code\tmp\coreapp> csc .\Program.cs .\A.cs .\B.cs
Microsoft (R) Visual C# Compiler version 1.3.1.60616
Copyright (C) Microsoft Corporation. All rights reserved.

PS C:\code\tmp\coreapp> .\Program.exe
6000003 A
6000004 B
PS C:\code\tmp\coreapp> csc .\Program.cs .\B.cs .\A.cs
Microsoft (R) Visual C# Compiler version 1.3.1.60616

PS C:\code\tmp\coreapp> .\Program.exe
6000003 B
6000004 A
```

>  I'd like to get a general sense of the conventions people use in representing durations like this. I'm a bit surprised that you chose M for minutes and m for months.

I pulled the month/minute case convention from the [strftime docs](https://docs.python.org/3/library/time.html#time.strftime). The positional encoding makes sense in 8601 because it is fully specified whereas this parser is meant as something of a practical convenience where it is not necessary to fully specify every token. 

> Are tenors ever expressed out of-order?

I have never seen a tenor expressed as more than one token. What we have now is a `relativedelta` parser which goes beyond just tenors. I think enforcing big-endian makes sense but unfortunately doesn't resolve the ambiguity of the string `1m` for example but it does create meaning for `1m 1m`. 

The question then is still what is the meaning of `1m`? I think falling back on the meaning defined by `strftime` is ok, not sure what other conventions there are that are context-free. 

> We'll still need some sort of token mapping, so that users can specify whether a string like 1.5M means "1.5 months" or "1.5 minutes", but it will be less important.

For convenience I did add a [token mapping argument](https://github.com/dateutil/dateutil/pull/454/files#diff-ed798069162e7ab7db9a45da6c4a076fR1520) to the function version of the parser with a default value.
