@andriikrupka (126,12) is interpreted as (Seq, Channel) by CoreML. CoreML treats Seq. dimension similar to a batch dimension hence it does not hard code it into the mlmodel. So the mlmodel for the input just says 12 corresponding to the number of channels. This way you can change the value of the sequence dimension at run time when you call predict in your app. At that time CoreML can be given a multi array of 5 dimensions, which maps to (Seq,B,C,H,W). You just need to create an array of shape (126,1,12,1,1) and pass it to the predict call. In fact you can also call predict on an input which has a different seq dimension (say 100 instead of 126) by passing in (100,1,12,1,1) sized input. You do not need to change the mlmodel. 

If in you case you only work on 126 length sequences, you can do a 2D conv such that the height dimension is 1 and the width dimension corresponds to the sequence dimension. Something like this:

inputlayer = Input(shape=(1,126,12))
model = BatchNormalization()(inputlayer)
model = Conv2D(16, (1,25), activation='relu')(model)
model = Flatten()(model)
model = Dense(10, activation='sigmoid')(model)
model = Model(inputs=inputlayer, outputs=model)
print(model.summary())

OK! I think this is good to be merged. I did some QA and it seems to be working as intended. You can currently undo and redo cell deletions, additions, and sticky celling. More actions can be added as it makes sense.

Here's a quick overview of how it works.

1. `SET_BACKWARD_CHECKPOINT` appends items to the `past` stack.
2. `triggerUndo` middleware calls the `SET_BACKWARD_CHECKPOINT` reducer on `store.document` if `action.type` is one of the actions that is considered undoable.
3. `UPDATE_DOCUMENT` overwrites `store.document` with the `newDocument` passed to it.
4. When the user hits undo, the last items is selected from the past stack and set to `UPDATE_DOCUMENT`. Then `SET_FORWARD_CHECKPOINT` is called to add it to the redo stack. Then `UNDO` is called which pops the last item off the stack.
5. When the user hits redo, the last item is selected from the future stack and passed to `UPDATED_DOCUMENT`.

Anyways, I think this is good for review and entry into master and we can iterate on it from there.

The problem is in [misc/json.c](https://github.com/mpv-player/mpv/blob/ea442fa047819ec2e48a3dbe8ea21959ac3d70b0/misc/json.c), it prints double via append functions from [misc/bstr.c](https://github.com/mpv-player/mpv/blob/ea442fa047819ec2e48a3dbe8ea21959ac3d70b0/misc/bstr.c), which finally prints your double via `vsnprintf`. It is a locale-aware function from standard library.

The issue could be solved either via setting locale to "POSIX" or "C" via `setlocale` (but it would affect locale for whole program and setting unsetting would be expensive) or use simple hack ","->"." replacement for json (as this is json-only problem - jsons are never locale-aware). This would be probably better, because you want to preserve locale everywhere else.

Just wanted to add the real scenario showing why solving this issue is important for us: on one server, we are currently using one "megacertificate" (from a different CA) for around 70 domain names. So first step in gradual migration to Let's Encrypt would be to issue the same certificate with the same 70 domain names, but this time from Let's Encrypt.

However if I wanted, as a second phase of migration to LE, to later "spin off" a certain group of domain names from this megacertificate to a certificate of its own, I would run into this issue. That is the main reason why I have not migrated yet to Let's Encrypt on that server: if I issued the megacertificate from Let's Encrypt, I could not later do this "spin off" of subsets of domain names; and I don't currently have the time to create right away all the "perfect sets" of domain names that should go into one certificate (I want to do it gradually).

The reason why we did not create those subsets already with our current CA is exactly because renewing certificates was a very demanding manual process for us, so we wanted to minimize the trouble of this process and have only one certificate to renew (and then are happy to be set for another year).

Also the reason to divide the domain name set into subsets with separate certificates is that often, these domain names have very little in common, and it simply does not feel proper to put them all together in one big certificate (sometimes even marketing reasons – to not associate two completely different websites – is relevant).

So, I am postponing migration to Let's Encrypt on that server until this issue is solved (or until I have a very clear idea about the perfect subsets of domain names, which I currently don't, and don't have time to create them at once, and even if I did, I would still like to be free to change my mind about them later, or correct a wrong "spin off").

One server where we already have Let's Encrypt deployed (and are loving it 😄 ) is one with just 3 certificates each with ~2-3 domain names, so there this issue was not a big deal.

```
    def reload_playlist(self):
        if self.closed:
            return

        self.reader.buffer.wait_free()
        log.debug("Reloading playlist")
        self.start_time = time()
        res = self.session.http.get(self.stream.url,
                                    exception=StreamError,
                                    retries=self.playlist_reload_retries,
                                    **self.reader.request_params)
        try:
            playlist = hls_playlist.load(res.text, res.url)
        except ValueError as err:
            raise StreamError(err)

        if playlist.is_master:
            raise StreamError("Attempted to play a variant playlist, use "
                              "'hlsvariant://{0}' instead".format(self.stream.url))

        if playlist.iframes_only:
            raise StreamError("Streams containing I-frames only is not playable")

        media_sequence = playlist.media_sequence or 0
        sequences = [Sequence(media_sequence + i, s)
                     for i, s in enumerate(playlist.segments)]

        if sequences:
            self.process_sequences(playlist, sequences)

    def process_sequences(self, playlist, sequences):
        first_sequence, last_sequence = sequences[0], sequences[-1]

        if first_sequence.segment.key and first_sequence.segment.key.method != "NONE":
            log.debug("Segments in this playlist are encrypted")

        self.playlist_changed = ([s.num for s in self.playlist_sequences] !=
                                 [s.num for s in sequences])
        self.playlist_reload_time = (playlist.target_duration or
                                     last_sequence.segment.duration)
        hls_latency = time() - self.start_time
        log.debug("HLS PL Latency: {}s", hls_latency)
```

```python
    async def sender(self):
        with self.client_sock:
```
This line in `sender` is telling Python that you want to close `client_sock` before `sender` exits, and the `async with trio.open_nursery()` block makes sure that `sender` has exited before returning. So that's what's closing the socket :-). You already have a `with` block for `client_sock` at the appropriate place in `parent`, so just delete the extra `with` and you should be good to go.
